item.content:encoded: This article contains everything I would find to be a great SCP. Good idea, great story, and I love the horrifying implications this holds. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. A good mix of disturbing in concept, depressing in execution, and shows that the Ethics Committee does not have final say on what happens concerning anomalies, something that I wish more articles would do.

item.title: Re: Minor formatting issue
item.content:encoded: O5-1 through O5-13 had always been the standard format for numbering the members of the O5 Council. No leading 0. It's fine if somebody wants to write it that way in their own articles, but I'm sticking with tradition on this one.

item.title: Minor formatting issue
item.content:encoded:
Hey, just a little thing I noticed in "Addendum 2410-C: O5 Council vote on the proposal raised in Report #46402716-A". Usually when someone has a numerical designation, and that designation can go into the double digits (e.g. O5-13), we would write two digits for all of them (e.g. O5-04 rather than O5-4).

It's a really minor thing, but for some reason, I'm wired to notice really minor things.

Happy SCP-ing!
Lots of love for this grizzly anomaly ;D

item.title: GRONK MOMENT
item.content:encoded: HMMM GRONK THINK MAYBE HE TRY WET GUTS NOW

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's the thing: amnestics are administered on a case by case basis. If a new situation shows up, they can administer it to a few individuals at a time.

The difference lies in that they realized there are 80,000 kids that need treatment. At that point, it's too much to handle all at once - compounded by the fact that mental health is a complicated matter and takes both time and resources to manage. Doing anything more to check all these kids into the clinic for mental therapy raises questions among the general public and threatens to break the Veil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Going by the comments I seem to have the opposite reaction of most. I really liked the bears and did not enjoy the politics at all. It doesn't even make sense, if the foundation has enough power to prevent anyone from remembering 80 thousand fleshbears, surely they can provide a little therapy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm I like the style of that vote tally but not the actual block itself. I'll see if I can't just rearrange things to make it clearer.

E: Good call ais, that does look a lot clearer!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall I like the article. It makes a simple anomaly with vivid and interesting effects. The focus on the ethical aspect of it was an enjoyable narrative take.

However I felt like the overseer/ec discussion was too terse and a bit predictable. I +1'd, but I would've enjoyed seeing a bit more there.

Nitpick, but the O5 vote panel should be collapsed to remove the intermediate space, like how SCP-001-EX does it. It would make it much easier to tell at a glance how the vote is going.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what's stopping the Foundation from using or making one more effective, other than costs?

I mean, reality is a big roadblock on that one. This article hinges on the premise that amnestics don't erase the trauma, just the memories. That means these miracle amnestics straight up don't exist in this universe because that's just not how they work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel as if after a while, therapy or not, many kids out there that were traumatized will become noticed, dammed if you do, dammed if you don't situation. Since there already giving them amnestics, what's stopping the Foundation from using or making one more effective, other than costs?
Sorry, my idealism is just speaking for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well done article. I'm always impressed by articles where the anomaly itself is so secondary to what is actually interesting about the article, it always feels fresh.

I think the dilemma is very well set up, with both sides being very understandable. On the one hand, the kids being adversely affected mentally by these bears is a damaging effect on normalcy, and so seems to be within the foundations purview. But it doesn't threaten the veil, and so is automatically a lower priority for the Foundation. Then, you have the fact that this has affected 80000 kids. That's a huge number, and for an organization with limited time and resources, I cannot fault those O5s for thinking a non-veil threatening issue like that isn't something the Foundation can justifiably try to address. If the anomaly were less common, if it were say 5000-6000 kids affected, I feel certain the measure would have passed. It just feels very well constructed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel this article should get more attention as it not just a monster Manuel or weird item description this shows tells a tale of how the foundation are only concerned with covering up anomalies. great job

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Motion has been DENIED per majority vote by the O5 Council

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made the mistake of reading this while holding Tubby. Now he and I both need therapy. +1, please pay our counseling bills, you monsters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I traumatized myself RL as a child accidentally disemboweling my favorite most loved stuffed animal and have struggled to this day with what I refer to as "Toy Story Syndrome."

It consists of intense acute mindblowing depression at the sight of a damaged soft toy. ANY damaged stuffed toy. (including dogs chew toys) It hits like a flipped switch and nothing stops it until 30-40 minutes it wears off on its own. There has been no therapy in over thirty years that has been effective to counter this. I glanced at the skip image and instinctively jerked my gaze away and locked my focus on the text of the skip. It almost got me, haha… almost…

Every hair on my body was standing on end while reading a skip that for the first time could have plausibly explained something weird about my everyday real life. I hate it and I love it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate your concern for bear kind. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely enjoyed the hell out of this. Please take my +1 in exchange for the entertainment.

Also, O5 did nothing wrong, EC are just a bunch of nerds. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
fuck it, blood in da bear

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was pretty much exactly what we tried to do here and we are super happy it seemed to have resonated with the readers. :D Thank you so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really felt in this piece that the Foundation is a complex bureaucratic system with many moving parts. Each part may be working toward the same goal, but the interpretation of that goal and the methods and means of reaching it vary wildly. Which is how the Foundation should be characterized — it feels more real, that way.

I will point out something (good) that I noticed about this piece. That being, while the grisly (no pun intended) nature of these teddy bears is the primary anomaly, their purpose to the Foundation is actually a tertiary concern. The bears are secondary to the children's distress, which is in turn a secondary roadblock to the Foundation's goal. While I felt the gruesomeness of the teddy bears in my gut in the Description, I'm more disturbed by the Foundation's cold approach to their duty by the end. Whether that was the intention or otherwise, it works.

Ramblings aside, yea, this is a good one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the Description, I believe that should be 'circulatory system' (not circular).

The way the final line is worded bugs me: "What happens in the light is none of our concern." When the character themself in the same letter says "Our purpose is to preserve normalcy", and "We can not jeapordize the Veil", that seems like concern for what happens in the light. I gather the sentiment is more along the lines of 'We die in the dark so they can live in the light; how they live there is none of our concern.' - though I guess that's a bit more wordy.

But overall I did like the article, so I'll still +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We die in the dark. What happens in the light is none of our concern.

Alright, but he didn't need to be that much of an asshole.

+1

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Criticism
item.content:encoded: Argh, I didn't catch that the first time! Excellent work with the image!

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Criticism
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the read and crit Tanhony!

Just a little note though:

That cute bear image can’t fool me, damnit…

Go look at it again, closer this time ;)

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Criticism
item.content:encoded:
Nice work. Very, very nice work.

Tanhony rambles
TURN IT OFF

A few notes:

Foundation agents embedded in toy manufacturing quality assurance contractors are to regularly sample teddy bears across all known manufacturers in search of a pattern for SCP-2410 manifestations.

This is a great hook in the containment procedures, coming right after the mentions of these things having to be incinerated. In the Foundation universe, bears are bad news more often than not, so it got me ready for something gruesome right off the bat. That cute bear image can’t fool me, damnit…

SCP-2410 refers to stuffed bears that have experienced an ectoentropic phenomenon wherein the materials that previously filled the bears are instantaneously replaced with anatomically correct internals for a bear of that size.

I always enjoy articles where the anomaly is just a thing that inexplicably happens - and in cases like this, where it’s something the Foundation has accepted just happens, even more so. It builds up the anomalous world the Foundation and groups like them operate in very nicely.

Upon being exposed to the environment, the instance will begin to decay at a rate that is typical for non-anomalous flesh and do not display further anomalous activity.

This is a really minor nitpick, but the phrasing here feels kind of awkward, mainly right at the end. I feel like ‘will not’ would read a little better than ‘do not’, especially with the phrasing already being established earlier in the sentence. ‘Being exposed’ might also read better as ‘exposure’, but that might just be personal preference.

Addendum 2410-A: Discovery Log

At first I wasn’t too sure about the discovery log here, and didn’t feel like it revealed anything new here that the description didn’t - but you obviously knew what you were doing here, as I realized when we got into the Ethics Committee stuff. Having an actual example of the human impact the anomaly is having was a good choice here.

We die in the dark. What happens in the light is none of our concern.

This is a fantastic closer for the article - I always enjoy seeing examination of what exactly the Foundation considers its mission to be, and articles with clashes between the EC and the O5 Council often look into that at least a little. This conclusion by the O51 is chillingly pragmatic, and perfectly in-character.

Rather than the Foundation existing to protect normalcy, this almost reads as it existing entirely outside normalcy, which is a very interesting direction to take.

+1

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to Tanhony or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

Footnotes
1. Although it's notably a very close decision in terms of votes!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that was the intention haha. It is not my personal headcanon but it is still fun to explore sometimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cold, but not cruel.

Though this is colder than usual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this. A simple core anomaly with a dark twist is a recipe for success. I think the response to the ethics committee went on a lite longer than it should have but it's not at all a deal breaker.

Good job guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well now i'm just depressed time to +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took a look at this when you sent it in the chat — it remains gross, upsetting, and great. +1

item.title: Author Post 3: Bottom Text Whee!!
item.content:encoded: God, this was fun to make. It feels so great to see just reminisce how we matured as writers, people, and friends and see how it reflected on this piece. Hope y'all like this scip!

item.title: Author Post 2: Electric Boogaloo
item.content:encoded: I think this thing was in the incubator for like 5 months, spanning 3 sandboxes. Both the article and we as authors changed and matured quite a lot in that time, but it is great to finally release this to the public! Hope you'll like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Will I do understand why some people would think that the effects of this SCP on children would fall into the jurisdiction of the Foundation, I completely understand and agree with the O5 council in this case, which I hate. Trying to mitigate this would be so prohibitively expensive and expansive that it would absolutely threaten normalcy.

There have been about 80,000 cases between 1979 and 2018, which is over 2,000 cases a year. By very loose estimates, assuming about half of them are caught in the factories and after that, a third of the anomalies are discovered by adults, that's still about 12,000 being found by children. Holding that 75% develop a mental illness based on this anomaly, that's 8,000 kids.

While the cost of therapy differs from country to country, I'm gonna take the lowball stat from the USA as our jumping-off point: $75 USD per hour. One hour a week, every week, is 4,000 per year. Let's assume they only need 5 years of therapy (A fantasy, I know) that's 20 grand per kid. That's at least 160 million dollars. That's too much money. O5-1 is right here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reasonable point. Unreasonable people exist in our universe, surely they must exist in our fictional universes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the idea here is to protect what is going on "in the light," so, it sorta is the Foundation's goddamn concern.

The real point of this article was to illustrate the philosophical differences between the generally conservative O5 Council and the rather liberal Ethics Committee of our headcanons, so the fact that this made you formulate and stand by an opinion here is honestly the whole point, and I consider that a success of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good enough for an upvote, IMO, but there's some wonky phrasing in a few places, most notably the closing letter:

Furthermore, the scope of this emotional trauma is far beyond the capabilities of what the Foundation is able to respond to.

We die in the dark. What happens in the light is none of our concern.

This isn't grammatically wrong, but the idea here is to protect what is going on "in the light," so, it sorta is the Foundation's goddamn concern.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exactly my kind of SCP: Strong core anomaly explained briefly, then the rest of the article focuses on a specific element of it's fall-out. Focusing on an internal struggle between the O5 council and the Ethics committee on the Foundations mission statement, and what "normalcy" means, was a good call.

My only two crits
My only two crits

1. "SCP-2410 refers to a type of stuffed bears that have experienced an ectoentropic phenomenon wherein the materials that previously filled the bears are instantaneously replaced with anatomically correct internals for a bear of that size."

The opening description is a bit dense. I'd split it up, and simplify the core premise. Something like

"They're teddy bears that have anatomically correct innards. Their previous stuffings, usually cotton or polymer, experience a spontaneous ectoentropic phenomenon causing blood, bone, etc. to manifest in their place." or some such.

2. The final response from O5-1 feels a bit long winded. There's some repetition, and a bit too much appeasement.

"We cannot jeopardize the Veil to deal with moral issues that fall outside of our jurisdiction and threaten our goals as an organization. These revisions, however well-intentioned, would cause more harm than good."

The second line could be removed entirely. The council already justified their position.

Overall, +1. I liked this one a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. The core anomaly is simple and grisly, the log is surprisingly effective despite its brusqueness, and the EthCom-Overseer debate is the most unique one I’ve seen in years.

item.title: Stole that first +1. Heyoooo
item.content:encoded:
I'll just give my thoughts on this as they were when I first read this because nothing has changed:

Absolutely f** joyless. I was thoroughly depressed the entire time I was reading it. From the terrifying and awful implications of the core concept to the emails, I did not know happiness. This trio of misery has constructed an unrelenting ride through the awful reality of the SCP Foundation. It was like the Green Room in text form. 11/10

item.title: A NaepicMightyCrash
item.content:encoded:
A bloody caricature to make whole
A squirming grisly jigsaw, detrital fragments fit so snug
That missing piece will leave you stumped
Totally desassembled, nicely sliced and diced
A cold mannequin once resembled
Real cranium teaser, carved from flesh and bone
So mystifying

Corporal Jigsore Quandry - Carcass

This was originally gonna be our third article each so we could all make author pages at the same time. That obviously fell through but hey, here it is, and it's a better article for the wait.

What used to be a simple little article about gory teddies turned into an analysis of Foundation internal politics, funny how that works sometimes.

Crashington and N_Aepic_Fael are dope as hell.

Also, take a closer look at that image if you haven't, cause Crash did an excellent job on it.

Click Here for more stuff by me!
